tldr: Tim Merlier wins the first full bunch sprint of the 2026 Tour, timing his move to perfection in the final 300m on Bordeaux's Garonne boulevard despite being boxed in at 600m to go. Søren Wærenskjold finishes second, Biniam Girmay third. GC unchanged — Pogačar retains yellow and the race heads into another flat day tomorrow.

narrative.headline: Merlier times his sprint to perfection on Bordeaux's grand boulevard
narrative.body:
Stage 7 crosses 175km of Landes and Gironde countryside from Hagetmau — following the Pyrenean queen stage and a GC reset of historic proportions — to a finish on Bordeaux's wide Garonne riverside boulevard, the most prestigious sprint venue remaining on the modern Tour calendar. The day's break forms early: Baptiste Veistroffer (Lotto Intermarché) and Jakub Otruba roll clear of the peloton through the pine-scented Landes forest, building a lead that the sprint teams keep under control as the Bordeaux wine country comes into view.

Uno-X inject drama at 22km to go when Anders Skaarseth and Jonas Abrahamsen jump clear in an attack designed to stress the leadouts before the sprint. Dylan van Baarle (Soudal Quick-Step) reacts immediately to close the gap, and by 20km to go Veistroffer and Otruba have given up the breakaway by unspoken agreement, rolling in as the peloton swallows them whole. By 10km the bunch is flying at 62km/h, UNO-X pushing to the front while EF attempt to punch clear of Soudal and NSN.

The technical run-in proves demanding: a dual-carriageway section with road furniture at 7.2km, then a series of roundabouts at 6km, the peloton snaking through narrow gaps. At 5km the sprint zone opens; Netcompany INEOS slide Josh Tarling to the front for their man. With 2km remaining, Mads Pedersen and Tim Merlier are both positioned near the front — Merlier still boxed in with 600m to go but tracking Jasper Philipsen with studied patience. In the final 300m Merlier times his move perfectly, threading through the messy, road-wide bunch to win by a clear margin. Wærenskjold outsprints the rest for second; Girmay takes third. A relief for Soudal Quick-Step after Pau, and a statement win for the Belgian on one of cycling's grandest finishing straights.

gcImpact.headline: GC unchanged — Pogačar retains yellow, sprint bonuses go to non-GC riders.
gcImpact.body: Stage 7 is a pure sprinters' day with no bearing on GC. All general classification contenders finished safely in the bunch. Time bonuses were awarded only to sprint specialists: Merlier (−10''), Wærenskjold (−6''), Girmay (−4''). GC after Stage 7: Tadej Pogačar (UAE Team Emirates XRG) leads. Jonas Vingegaard (Team Visma | Lease a Bike) 2nd at +2:42. Isaac del Toro (UAE Team Emirates XRG) 3rd at +3:27. Remco Evenepoel (Red Bull - BORA - hansgrohe) 4th at +3:30. Juan Ayuso (Lidl-Trek) 5th at +3:34. Paul Seixas (Decathlon CMA CGM Team) 6th at +3:55. Florian Lipowitz (Red Bull - BORA - hansgrohe) 7th at +4:00. Lenny Martinez (Bahrain Victorious) 8th at +4:21. Mattias Skjelmose (Lidl-Trek) 9th at +4:57.

riderPerformances.name: Tim Merlier
riderPerformances.narrative: Merlier's Soudal Quick-Step squad controls the race through the final 20km, with van Baarle neutralising the Uno-X counter at 22km to go. Merlier finds himself boxed in approaching 600m but stays calm — tracking Philipsen's wheel and waiting for the gap to open. He unleashes his sprint in the final 300m on the Garonne boulevard, winning comfortably despite the messy bunch. He credits his team's positioning and the Wolfpack's patient work through the day. A major confidence boost for Soudal Quick-Step, who were shut out in the Pau sprint two days prior.

keyMoments.what: Van Baarle closes down the Uno-X counter-attack for Soudal
Boxed in at 600m to go; holds composure tracking Philipsen
Wins the sprint in the final 300m on the Garonne boulevard

riderPerformances.name: Søren Wærenskjold
riderPerformances.narrative: Wærenskjold delivers an excellent second place for Uno-X in Bordeaux, outsprinting the rest of the field after Merlier takes the win. The Norwegian, who had earlier seen his teammates Skaarseth and Abrahamsen attempt a 22km-to-go counter-attack, demonstrates strong finishing speed to hold off Girmay for the runner-up spot.

keyMoments.what: 2nd in the bunch sprint behind Merlier

riderPerformances.name: Biniam Girmay
riderPerformances.narrative: Girmay takes third place for NSN Cycling Team in the Bordeaux sprint, his team's best result of the week-one sprinting opportunities. He had been flagged before the stage as a key contender on a finish suited to his punchy sprint style, and delivers on that billing with a podium finish.

keyMoments.what: 3rd in Bordeaux sprint for NSN Cycling Team

riderPerformances.name: Jasper Philipsen
riderPerformances.narrative: Philipsen finishes 5th in Bordeaux, a below-par result for the Belgian, who Merlier was watching closely throughout the final kilometre. Merlier credited keeping an eye on Philipsen even while boxed in at 600m, suggesting Alpecin's sprinter was the pre-race favourite. A day where his lead-out didn't deliver the position he needed.

keyMoments.what: Tracked by Merlier through final 600m; 5th in sprint

riderPerformances.name: Mads Pedersen
riderPerformances.narrative: Pedersen pushes to the front in the final 2km in his role as green jersey holder and sprinting hopeful, but does not place in the top 5. He retains the points classification jersey heading into Stage 8. With the green jersey competition ongoing and the next sprint opportunities coming on Stages 8 and 11, Pedersen's focus is on accumulating points rather than stage wins in every finish.

keyMoments.what: Pushes to the front in final 2km; finishes in the bunch

quotes.speaker: Tim Merlier
quotes.text: I didn't think I'd timed my sprint to perfection, with the bunch messy across the road in the closing metres. But I'm so glad to see the Wolfpack win here — it means a lot after Pau. I'd kept an eye on Jasper all the way even while boxed in.
